Iran hat die Straße von Hormus erneut für geschlossen erklärt, bis auf Weiteres. Der Schiffsverkehr liegt bei praktisch null.

Beide Seiten greifen jetzt zivile Infrastruktur an. Kuwait meldet den zweiten Treffer auf seine Kraft- und Entsalzungsanlage innerhalb von 48 Stunden, dazu brennende Treibstofftanks am Flughafen. 90% des kuwaitischen Trinkwassers kommen aus Entsalzung. Die USA treffen Brücken, Energieanlagen und den Hafen Chabahar.

Kuwait kürzt vorsorglich die eigene Ölproduktion.

Der eigentliche Schock läuft aber über Gas: Katar hat als erster Golfstaat sämtliche Schifffahrt gestoppt. Ras Laffan, das größte LNG-Exportzentrum der Welt, liefert nicht mehr.

Europa trifft das mitten in der Einspeichersaison. TTF notiert über 56 Euro/MWh, plus 29% allein im Juli. Jede Woche ohne Katar-LNG macht die Speicherfüllung vor dem Winter teurer.

Der Ölmarkt zeigt die Knappheit längst in den Produkten: Diesel-Crack-Spread auf Rekordhoch bei 66 Dollar pro Barrel. Brent schloss am Freitag bei 88,10 Dollar, plus 4,6%.

Und die nächste Eskalationsstufe ist bereits definiert: Iran hat die Houthis angewiesen, sich bereitzuhalten, das Rote Meer zu schließen, falls Washington die iranische Strominfrastruktur angreift. Das Weiße Haus prüft parallel den Einsatz von Bodentruppen.

Zwei Chokepoints gleichzeitig, Öl und Gas gleichzeitig, mitten in der Einspeichersaison. Das ist das Szenario, das gerade auf dem Tisch liegt.

⚠️Watch this week: Monday oil price action, EIA on Wednesday (SPR + Cushing), and whether diesel tightness starts feeding back into crude more visibly.

With the current Middle East escalation (2 US servicemen KIA in Jordan this weekend, US already hitting back) and Russia’s diesel export ban still in force until 31 July after Ukraine’s deep refinery strikes, the most likely path is continued SPR draws, Cushing staying pinned near the operational floor, and diesel crack spreads widening further.

No immediate off-ramp in sight on either front. Baseline remains thin usable buffers + two live supply disruptions through at least Q3.

Everyone wants to know how to make money trading options.

Far fewer ask the more important question:

How do you preserve capital when the market regime changes?

Social media has created the illusion that professional trading is about maximizing returns.

It isn't.

It's about managing risk through changing market conditions.

Most accounts showcase their winners.

Very few publish the periods when their portfolio is under pressure.

Those periods matter the most because they reveal whether a strategy is robust or simply benefited from a favorable market.

My own portfolio hasn't been immune.

Over the past few weeks, several cash-secured puts on high-conviction positions across semiconductors, AI infrastructure, and energy were assigned.

That wasn't a failure of the strategy.

It was an expected outcome.

Assignment is simply another stage in the trade.

Rather than viewing it as a loss, I now own businesses I'm comfortable holding over the long term while generating additional income through covered calls.

This is what I refer to as opportunistic equity ownership.

If I'm willing to own the asset anyway, I'd rather be paid to enter the position than purchase it outright at the prevailing market price.

The more interesting question, however, is how to position ourselves from here.

Over recent weeks, several macro signals have begun to diverge from the prevailing market narrative.

Refined product markets remain firm, with diesel crack spreads rising in price, continuing to suggest underlying inflationary pressure.

Headline inflation has moderated, but commodity markets often move ahead of the economic data that eventually reflects those changes.

Markets rarely reprice overnight.

They adjust gradually as new information is incorporated into expectations.

That environment calls for a different approach than the one that worked during the recent advance.

When uncertainty rises, preserving optionality becomes more valuable than maximizing exposure.

I've gradually reduced directional risk while increasing the proportion of capital held in cash.

For the equity exposure I continue to hold, I've introduced defined-risk hedges through out-of-the-money SPX call credit spreads.

The objective isn't to predict a market decline.

It's to improve the portfolio's payoff profile if upside momentum begins to fade.

Professional options portfolios are rarely built around a single forecast.

They are constructed so that multiple outcomes remain manageable.

That's an important distinction.

From a premium-selling perspective, the current environment still appears favorable.

Selling defined-risk SPX call credit spreads in the 30 to 45 DTE window allows time decay to work while maintaining clearly defined downside.

In many cases, I prefer realizing gains before expiration, around the 14 DTE mark, when gamma risk begins to accelerate and the risk-reward profile changes.

The objective isn't to maximize every trade.

It's to maximize the consistency of the process.

At the shorter end of the curve, I continue to trade 0DTE SPX credit spreads.

Contrary to popular belief, 0DTE options are not inherently reckless.

They simply require a different risk framework.

With clearly defined exits, disciplined sizing, and an understanding of intraday market structure, they offer immediate feedback, efficient capital deployment, and eliminate overnight event risk.

They are a tool.

Nothing more.

Nothing less.

As we move deeper into earnings season, corporate guidance, inflation data, and central bank communication will likely determine whether recent market leadership broadens or begins to weaken.

Until that picture becomes clearer, my priority remains unchanged:

Preserve capital. Generate consistent cash flow. Stay flexible enough to adapt when the market regime inevitably changes.

The best traders aren't the ones who predict every move.

They're the ones who survive long enough to benefit from the opportunities that follow.

⚡ Gasoline stocks in ARA have fallen to their lowest seasonal levels in many years, with cracks exploding higher once again. Continued inventory depletion across key trading hubs sustains powerful bullish tailwinds in the refined products market.
